label: SES-053 YAML schema v1.2 Category 6 reordering: 1 session, 2 decisions (DEC-153, DEC-154), 0 planning items, 2 references

session.title: YAML schema Category 6 (Role-Based Access Control) reordered across v1.2 and v1.3; gap-analysis Section 6 amended and v1.2 drafting kickoff committed
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai ARCHITECTURE-mode conversation that opened against the open question of how to express deployment-time security declaratively in CRM Builder's YAML schema. The conversation walked the design space in two consequential decisions: first, the fundamental fork between definitions-only and definitions-plus-assignments scope for the YAML (resolved as Option A, definitions only); second, the reordering of Category 6 of the gap analysis between schema v1.2 and v1.3 (resolved as Option C, scope-level + teams in v1.2, field-level deferred to v1.3). Between decisions the conversation produced a strawman security block grounded in a Mentor role for Cleveland Business Mentors, then ran a code-verification pass against the actual loader and validator in espo_impl/core/config_loader.py to confirm the existing top-level key set and the absence of any security/roles/teams recognition. After the second decision locked, the gap-analysis Section 6 was rewritten end-to-end to express the new five-part v1.2 scope (Parts A–E) and the deferred v1.3 scope (old Parts B and C), with Q1–Q3 amended and Q7–Q8 added. A new UPDATE-PROMPT was then authored as the kickoff for the next conversation (drafting the operator-facing reference sections of app-yaml-schema.md for v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E). All three documents were committed and pushed; a stale commit-SHA reference in the kickoff prompt was discovered post-rebase and corrected in a follow-up commit. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Lets discuss deploying security with YAML".

Landscape framing. Opened by mapping the EspoCRM security model into its constituent layers (roles, teams, scope-level access, field-level access, assignment permission, portal roles, user-to-role and user-to-team assignments) and naming the one fork that needed to settle before any schema work could begin: whether the YAML owns role and team *definitions* only or *definitions + assignments*. Side-flagged the existing memory note that YAML schema Category 6 was deferred to v1.2, with verification of current status to come once a direction was chosen.

Decision 1 — definitions-only security model (Option A). Doug chose Option A: YAML defines roles and teams; user-to-role and user-to-team assignments are runtime data managed in the target CRM UI. Locked as DEC-153. Rationale: matches the prevailing IAM industry pattern (policy as code, identity membership as runtime state); avoids requiring a YAML edit and Configure run for every new user. The CBM scenario of a new mentor onboarded mid-cycle was the concrete motivator.

Portal and field-level scope confirmation. Confirmed no portal in CBM's plans, so Portal Roles drop from v1.0 scope. Confirmed (by default, not reopened) that the original Category 6 field-level deferral to v1.2 held at this point in the conversation; this would be revisited at Decision 2.

Strawman drafting. Produced a strawman security block grounded in a CBM Mentor role: top-level `security:` containing `teams:` (Mentors, Administrators, Fundraising) and `roles:` with one fully-realized Mentor role showing `scope_access:` per entity (c-Engagement, c-Session, Contact, Account) using the EspoCRM grain (create as yes/no; read/edit/delete/stream as all/team/own/no), plus `system_permissions:` covering assignment_permission, user_permission, export, mass_update, audit_log. Flagged three implementation concerns: YAML boolean coercion on bare `no`; the limit of EspoCRM's role scope levels for expressing linked-record access (Mentor → Contact via Engagement); naming convention for built-in vs. custom entities. Doug approved the structure and noted values can be revised against the final security design.

Code verification — does the YAML processor handle any of this today? Conversation pivoted from PRD/schema work to code investigation per the working pattern that mandates verifying behavior against the actual codebase. Doug confirmed crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md as the reference. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo with the project's standard sparse pattern; read CLAUDE.md head; inspected espo_impl/core/config_loader.py for top-level key recognition; inspected the validate_program() function for unknown-key enforcement; searched for any role or team manager files in espo_impl/core/ and confirmed none exist; located the existing Category 6 plan in PRDs/product/yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md and read the four-part structure (Parts A–D, with Part D added 05-03-26 covering panel and layout role-aware visibility).

Gap-analysis findings reported to Doug. (1) Loader recognizes exactly five top-level keys: version, description, content_version, entities, relationships. No security:, roles:, or teams: handling. (2) validate_program() applies no top-level key whitelist; an unknown top-level key is silently ignored (no error, no warning, no effect). (3) No role manager, team manager, or API client wrappers for /api/v1/Role or /api/v1/Team exist. Greenfield. (4) EspoCRM's REST API exposes Role and Team as first-class CRUD entities — not in the no-public-write-path list (which contains only saved views, duplicate checks, workflows). (5) The existing v1.2 plan in the gap analysis is field-level-centric; it covers Part A (roles declaration), Part B (field-level permissions), Part C (permission presets), and Part D (panel/layout role-aware visibility). Critically, it covers no teams concept at all and no scope-level entity access (CRUD per role at all/team/own/no). The strawman from earlier in the conversation tackled exactly what was missing from the existing plan. The two scopes are complementary, not competing.

Decision 2 — Category 6 reordering (Option C). Surfaced the gap between the existing v1.2 plan (field-level + presets) and the strawman scope (scope-level + teams + system permissions) as a consequential decision passing the two-part test. Three options presented: (A) narrow v1.2 — ship the existing plan only, scope-level and teams remain manual config; (B) expanded v1.2 — ship both, double the surface area; (C) reorder — ship scope-level + teams as v1.2, field-level shifts to v1.3. Recommended C with one-line rationale that scope-level access is more universal (every role needs entity-level CRUD bounds; only some roles need field-level overrides) and that field-level was acknowledged in the original plan as needing additional pilot input. Doug chose C. Locked as DEC-154.

Gap-analysis Section 6 amendment. With Option C locked, the gap-analysis Section 6 was rewritten end-to-end in a single commit (eb1d7e7). New five-part v1.2 structure: Part A — Roles declaration (largely preserved from original, name-as-identity, persona metadata); Part B — Teams declaration (NEW); Part C — Scope-level entity access (NEW) with whitelist semantics, vocabulary documented, YAML boolean coercion called out; Part D — System permissions (NEW); Part E — Panel and layout role-aware visibility (was original Part D, renumbered, content preserved). New deferred-to-v1.3 section covers old Part B (field-level permissions) and old Part C (permission presets). Q1 amended (still defer to v1.2, with explicit part split); Q2 amended (file renamed from programs/roles.yaml to programs/security.yaml to reflect roles + teams); Q3 amended (presets deferred to v1.3); Q7 added (user-to-role and user-to-team assignments are out of YAML scope, capturing the DEC-153 outcome at the gap-analysis level); Q8 added (whitelist semantics for scope_access: omission denies). Change log entry recorded at the top of the document.

Next-step kickoff prompt. UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md authored at PRDs/product/ as the kickoff for the next Claude.ai conversation, which will draft the operator-facing reference sections of app-yaml-schema.md covering v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E. The kickoff names three decisions likely to surface in that session (section placement, file-type concept, deploy-ordering expression) and constrains the session to documentation drafting only (no code, no Claude Code prompt authoring, no design extension beyond what gap-analysis Section 6 specifies). Initial commit at f152181.

Post-rebase SHA fix. Push of the two pending commits (gap analysis amendment, UPDATE-PROMPT) hit a non-fast-forward error; git pull --rebase incorporated remote changes (originating from a different recent conversation's commits), which rewrote the local commit SHAs. The kickoff prompt's `Created in:` line still referenced the pre-rebase SHA 3d883b9; corrected to eb1d7e7 in a follow-up commit at 4ff2e43.

Close-out artifact authoring. This payload (ses_053.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-053.md) authored at conversation close. The session record is bundled into the payload per the operative SES-046-through-SES-052 precedent. Identifier check at close-out time against the v2 database snapshot (db-export/sessions.json, decisions.json, planning_items.json): latest applied SES is SES-052; next-available is SES-053. Latest applied DEC is DEC-152; next-available is DEC-153 and DEC-154 for the two decisions. Latest applied PI is PI-023; no new planning item is created in this session (the next-conversation kickoff is committed as a work artifact, not deferred as an open question). The apply prompt's pre-flight verifies these heads are still uncontested at run time.
session.artifacts_produced: Three files committed and pushed to the crmbuilder repo in this conversation: (1) PRDs/product/yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md (modified — Section 6 rewritten end-to-end into the new five-part v1.2 structure plus deferred-to-v1.3 section; change log entry added; Q1–Q3 amended; Q7–Q8 added; net diff +295 / -133 lines; committed at eb1d7e7). (2) PRDs/product/UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md (new — kickoff prompt for the next-conversation drafting of app-yaml-schema.md v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E sections; committed at f152181). (3) Follow-up SHA correction commit at 4ff2e43 fixing a stale pre-rebase commit-SHA reference inside the kickoff prompt. Plus this close-out payload (close-out-payloads/ses_053.json) and the companion apply prompt (prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-053.md), committed together as the close-out artifacts. Two decision records (DEC-153, DEC-154) and two references land via this payload's apply prompt. No source code modified; this is a planning conversation with a documentation-edits side-track. No CBM repo edits. No PRDs touched outside the two named above.
session.in_flight_at_end: The next Claude.ai conversation opens against PRDs/product/UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md (committed at f152181, SHA corrected at 4ff2e43). That session drafts the operator-facing reference sections of app-yaml-schema.md covering v1.2 Category 6 Parts A–E (Roles declaration, Teams declaration, scope-level entity access, system permissions, panel/layout role-aware visibility). Per the kickoff, the session is expected to surface three consequential decisions before drafting begins: section placement in app-yaml-schema.md (new top-level section vs. slotted under existing structure), file-type concept (new program-file type for security vs. extension of existing single program-file type to allow optional roles:/teams: top-level keys), and deploy-ordering expression (schema-level constraint vs. pipeline convention vs. loader-discovery rule). Downstream of the schema-spec session, the v1.2 Category 6 implementation prompt series for Claude Code (analogous to v1.1's archived A–H series at PRDs/_archive/yaml-schema-prompts/) covers loader extensions parsing the new top-level keys, validator extensions enforcing whitelist semantics and role-name cross-references, new deploy managers (role_manager.py and team_manager.py) wired into the Configure pipeline ahead of fields and relationships, API client extensions for /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team CRUD, and test coverage matching the v1.1 series pattern. The v1.3 deferred field-level + presets work (old Parts B and C of the original Category 6 plan) is queued behind the v1.2 implementation series; the MR-pilot's two access-control items (MR-MC-AC-001/002) remain on the MANUAL CONFIGURATION list through v1.2 release and ship at v1.3.

decisions.title: YAML security scope: definitions only, not user assignments
decisions.context: Opening question of how to express deployment-time security in CRM Builder's YAML schema. The fundamental fork: does the YAML own role and team *definitions* (the structures — what each role can access, which teams exist) AND user-to-role and user-to-team *assignments* (the membership state — which users belong to which roles and teams), or only definitions, with assignments treated as runtime data managed in the target CRM UI? Both options are defensible: the definitions-plus-assignments model fully describes the running security posture from the deployment artifact, but requires a YAML edit and Configure run for every new user. The definitions-only model leaves runtime user management in the CRM UI but matches the industry pattern of treating IAM policy as code and identity membership as runtime state.
decisions.decision: **Adopt definitions-only.** YAML declares role and team structures and their permissions (scope-level access, system permissions, field-level permissions when v1.3 ships). User-to-role and user-to-team assignments are runtime data managed exclusively in the target CRM UI; they are not represented in any YAML file and not touched by the deploy pipeline. Adding a new user (e.g., onboarding a new mentor at CBM) does not require a YAML edit or a Configure run — the user is created in the CRM UI and assigned to roles and teams in the CRM UI.
decisions.rationale: Matches the prevailing IAM industry pattern (policy as code, identity membership as runtime state). For CBM's operational reality (mentors onboarded continuously as recruitment progresses), the alternative would require a YAML commit, push, and Configure run for every new mentor — operationally heavier than the structural benefit it provides. The structure of the security posture is fully described in the YAML (which roles exist, what each can do, which teams exist); the membership state lives in the CRM where it changes most frequently and where the CRM UI already provides the management surface. Decouples the deployment artifact's churn rate from the user population's churn rate.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option B — definitions + assignments in YAML.** The deployment artifact fully describes the running security posture; new users require a YAML edit and a Configure run. Defensible for small, stable user bases where 'redeploy on new user' might even be a feature (auditable user lifecycle in git). Rejected because the operational cost compounds quickly (every CBM mentor recruitment adds churn), the YAML loses its 'structure declaration' purity by mixing in identity data, and the industry pattern is clear that policy-as-code stops at the role boundary rather than extending to membership.
decisions.consequences: Recorded as Q7 in the amended gap-analysis Section 6 (commit eb1d7e7). The role and team declarations in programs/security.yaml describe structure only — no members:, users:, or assignments: blocks on roles or teams. When the v1.2 implementation lands (Parts A–E per DEC-154), the role_manager.py and team_manager.py deploy managers create and update Role and Team records via /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team but do not touch the User entity or any user-to-role / user-to-team linkage tables. Operators manage user lifecycle (creation, role assignment, team membership, deactivation) through the target CRM's standard administrative UI. The definitions-only posture is final for v1.2 and v1.3; revisiting requires a new decision.

decisions.title: Category 6 reordering: v1.2 ships scope-level access + teams (Parts A–E); v1.3 ships field-level + presets
decisions.context: The existing Category 6 plan in PRDs/product/yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md targeted YAML schema v1.2 for the entire Role-Based Access Control category — Part A (roles declaration), Part B (field-level permissions, the original target driven by MR-MC-AC-001/002), Part C (permission presets), and Part D (panel and layout role-aware visibility, added 05-03-26). Verification against the actual codebase in this conversation confirmed: (1) the loader at espo_impl/core/config_loader.py recognizes only version, description, content_version, entities, and relationships — no security-related top-level keys; (2) validate_program() applies no top-level key whitelist, so an unknown security: block parses as a silent no-op; (3) no role manager, team manager, or API client wrappers exist; (4) the EspoCRM REST API exposes Role and Team as first-class CRUD entities at /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team. The strawman drafted earlier in the conversation covered scope-level entity access (CRUD per role at all/team/own/no), teams, and system permissions — none of which appeared in the existing v1.2 plan. The existing v1.2 plan covered field-level access; the strawman did not. The two scopes are complementary, not competing.
decisions.decision: **Adopt Option C — scope-level + teams as v1.2; field-level shifts to v1.3.** Category 6 splits across two schema versions. v1.2 ships five parts: Part A — Roles declaration (kept from original plan); Part B — Teams declaration (NEW); Part C — Scope-level entity access with whitelist semantics (NEW); Part D — System permissions including assignment_permission, user_permission, export, mass_update, audit_log (NEW); Part E — Panel and layout role-aware visibility (renumbered from original Part D, content preserved). v1.3 ships the deferred field-level read/edit permissions (was original Part B) and named permission presets (was original Part C). Dedicated security file is renamed from programs/roles.yaml to programs/security.yaml to reflect the roles + teams scope.
decisions.rationale: Scope-level access and teams are universal to every role in every domain (every role needs entity-level CRUD bounds; every team-scope access level requires a team to exist) and translate directly to target-CRM role and team primitives without needing condition-expression infrastructure. Field-level access is the harder problem (state-dependent permissions, asymmetric per-role per-field overrides, cross-CRM mapping ambiguity, condition-expression dependency) and the original plan's own commentary acknowledged that field-level 'deserves careful design across at least one more pilot domain before commitment.' Reordering ships meaningful security capability now (the Mentor role can be expressed in YAML for the first time, even if imperfectly) instead of waiting on the full field-level design. The MR-pilot's two field-level items (MR-MC-AC-001/002 — ten admin-only fields and ten state-dependent mentor-editable fields) remain Manual Configuration through v1.2 and ship at v1.3.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option A — narrow v1.2.** Ship the existing plan (Parts A–D as originally drafted) without scope-level or teams. Scope-level access and teams remain Manual Configuration line items. Rejected because the deployment artifact cannot describe a meaningful security posture without scope-level access — the Mentor role cannot be expressed at all (every role would either see all entities or none). The cost of waiting on field-level to ship anything at all is too high. **Option B — expanded v1.2.** Ship the existing plan plus scope-level access plus teams in a single v1.2 release. Defensible but doubles the v1.2 surface area, delays delivery of either piece, and forces field-level into a release before the additional pilot input the original plan flagged as needed has accrued. Rejected on delivery speed and on the principle that field-level deserves more pilot data before commitment.
decisions.consequences: Gap-analysis Section 6 amended end-to-end (commit eb1d7e7) — Q1, Q2, Q3 amended; Q7 and Q8 added; five v1.2 parts (A–E) and a deferred-to-v1.3 section. UPDATE-PROMPT for the next conversation authored at PRDs/product/UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md (commit f152181; SHA fix at 4ff2e43) directing the drafting of app-yaml-schema.md v1.2 sections covering Parts A–E. The v1.2 implementation prompt series for Claude Code (analogous to v1.1's archived A–H series at PRDs/_archive/yaml-schema-prompts/) is the downstream artifact for the conversation after that — covering loader extensions, validator extensions, role_manager.py and team_manager.py, and API client extensions for /api/v1/Role and /api/v1/Team. The v1.3 field-level + presets work is queued behind the v1.2 implementation series. MR-pilot items MR-MC-AC-001/002 remain on the MANUAL CONFIGURATION list through v1.2 release.
